metadata.summary: Canonical Go project directory structure: cmd/, internal/, pkg/, migrations/ — one agreed-upon layout that cuts bikeshedding and scales from small service to multi-binary monorepo.

content.title: Go Standard Layout — Directory Structure
content.summary: Organize every Go service under a four-directory skeleton: cmd/ (binaries), internal/ (private app code), pkg/ (public libraries), migrations/ (schema files). The layout is a community convention — not enforced by the toolchain — so teams must agree on it once and commit it to docs.

why.p: Without an agreed-upon layout, package placement is decided per-PR, accumulating in a different shape each time. The Go community converged on the golang-standards/project-layout convention because it maps naturally to how the Go compiler resolves import paths: internal/ makes everything under it importable only within the module, cmd/ makes the entry-point intent obvious, and pkg/ signals deliberate external API.

The layout is a navigability contract: a developer (or agent) landing on an unfamiliar service knows where handlers live, where domain models live, and where the database migration runner is, without reading any documentation.

list.item: Greenfield Go services where the team wants a single, agreed-upon directory convention to cut bikeshedding about "where does this go?"
Mid-size services with multiple binaries (cmd/api, cmd/worker, cmd/migrate) sharing a domain core under internal/.
Monorepos that need to publish reusable libraries via pkg/ while keeping app code private under internal/.
Onboarding-heavy teams where a navigable layout matters more than micro-optimisations to package shape.
Refactoring a "single-package" Go app that grew past ~3k LOC and now wants explicit handler/service/repository seams.
Tiny CLIs (500 LOC or fewer, one binary) — main.go plus a couple of files is sufficient; the layout is a tax.
Library-only modules — the golang-standards/project-layout doc explicitly states it is not for libraries.
Teams that have already converged on a different convention (e.g., Domain-driven internal/context/... package-by-feature) — switching mid-flight churns the diff for no win.
Hyper-optimised single-package code where every package boundary is a string-allocating call.
Educational or demo repos where the layout obscures the lesson — keep flat.

section.name: canonical-layout
section.p: The standard skeleton for a Go service or multi-binary project:

example.title: Standard project tree
example.code: project/ ├── cmd/ │ └── api/ │ └── main.go # Entry point ├── internal/ │ ├── handler/ # HTTP handlers │ ├── service/ # Business logic │ ├── repository/ # Data access │ ├── model/ # Domain models │ ├── middleware/ # HTTP middleware │ └── config/ # Configuration ├── pkg/ # Public packages (optional — add only when you have a real external API) ├── migrations/ # Database migrations ├── go.mod └── go.sum
example.note: pkg/ is optional. Do not add it until you have a documented external consumer.

rule.statement: cmd/name is wiring only — read config, build dependencies, start the server. No business logic, no goroutines spawning workers (compose them in services).
rule.rationale: Keeping cmd/ thin makes every entry point a readable composition root and prevents business logic from being unreachable by tests.

rule.statement: internal/ is the default home for all new code. Promote to pkg/ only when a documented external consumer exists.
rule.rationale: internal/ enforces the Go compiler's import restriction, preventing accidental external imports before the API is stable.

rule.statement: Small, focused packages. Aim for one responsibility per package (handlers, services, repos). A long file is acceptable; a long package is not.
rule.rationale: Package-level cohesion makes dependencies explicit; a package that mixes HTTP, persistence, and domain logic forces callers to import everything.

rule.statement: Do not export what you do not have to. Lowercase types in internal/ keep the package surface small.
rule.rationale: Unexported types are impossible to depend on from outside, which gives the author freedom to refactor without breaking callers.

rule.statement: Migrations versioned as migrations/NNNN_description.up.sql + .down.sql. Apply via cmd/migrate or golang-migrate.
rule.rationale: Numbered migration files give both humans and automated runners a deterministic apply order and a safe rollback path.

rule.statement: A Makefile or Taskfile.yml documents canonical commands (make test, make lint, make migrate-up). Commit it to the repo.
rule.rationale: Without a canonical command reference, every developer and every agent invents a different invocation; the Makefile is the single source of truth for local dev workflow.

section.name: handler-example
section.p: Handlers live in internal/handler/ and depend on service interfaces (defined in internal/service/), never directly on repositories. The handler receives a context.Context from the request and passes it downstream.

example.title: HTTP handler in internal/handler/
example.note: Handler depends on service.UserService interface, not on a concrete repository. The layout works with chi/echo/net/http too — do not mix routers across handler files.

antipattern.description: pkg/ cargo-cult: adding pkg/ from the layout template without anything to publish, so it becomes a second random bin of utilities.
antipattern.reason: Skip pkg/ until you have a real external API. Empty or utility-only pkg/ trees signal layout-as-ritual rather than layout-as-architecture.

antipattern.description: internal/ tree too deep: internal/service/order/v2/usecases/... defeats the navigability the layout was supposed to bring.
antipattern.reason: Depth beyond three levels negates the benefit of a flat layout; flatten or use package-by-feature inside internal/ instead.

antipattern.description: Config sprawl: internal/config becomes a god struct that depends on every package's options.
antipattern.reason: Each package should own its own config struct; internal/config should compose them, not import them all directly.

antipattern.description: Migrations + binary coupling: migrations/ lives at the root but there is no cmd/migrate, so the dance of applying migrations is undiscoverable.
antipattern.reason: Without cmd/migrate and a Makefile target, every developer invents a different apply command; this silently skips migrations in CI.

checklist.check: cmd/ contains only wiring: config load, dependency construction, server start.
internal/ contains all application code not intended for external import.
pkg/ exists only if at least one documented external consumer exists.
Makefile or Taskfile.yml documents test, lint, and migrate-up commands.
No deeply nested internal/ paths (more than 3 levels).
